title: Problem 1 (Erdős-Sands-Sauer-Woodrow)
statement_text: For each $n$, is there a (least) positive integer $f(n)$ so that every finite tournament whose edges are coloured with $n$ colours contains a set $S$ of $f(n)$ vertices with the property that for every vertex $u$ not in $S$ there is a monochromatic path from $u$ to a vertex of $S$?
context_text: In [15] Sands, Sauer and Woodrow asked this question, also due to Erdős. It concerns the existence of a bounded-size dominating set reachable by monochromatic paths in any $n$-edge-coloured tournament. The transitive closure of each colour class is a quasi-order, leading to the equivalent formulation as Conjecture 2.
paper_contribution: Proves the existence of such $f(n)$ by establishing $\gamma(T) = O(\ln(2k) \cdot k^{k+2})$ for complete multidigraphs whose arcs are the union of $k$ quasi-orders (Theorem 6), which implies the tournament case.
paper_title: A proof of the Erdős-Sands-Sauer-Woodrow conjecture

title: Conjecture 2 (Erdős-Sands-Sauer-Woodrow, quasi-order formulation)
statement_text: For every $k$, there exists an integer $f(k)$ such that if $T$ is a complete multidigraph whose arcs are the union of $k$ quasi-orders, then $\gamma(T) \leq f(k)$.
context_text: This is the restatement of Problem 1 in terms of quasi-orders and complete multidigraphs. The main goal of the paper is to prove this conjecture. It directly implies that every finite subset $X$ of $\mathbb{R}^d$ can be covered by $f(d)$ $X$-boxes, as shown by Gyárfás and Pálvölgyi.
paper_contribution: Proved in full: Theorem 6 establishes $\gamma(T) = O(\ln(2k) \cdot k^{k+2})$, giving an explicit bound for $f(k)$.
paper_title: A proof of the Erdős-Sands-Sauer-Woodrow conjecture

title: Conjecture 3 (General Sands-Sauer-Woodrow Conjecture)
statement_text: For every $k$, there exists an integer $f(k)$ such that if $D$ is a multidigraph whose arcs are the union of $k$ quasi-orders, then $D$ has a dominating set which is the union of $f(k)$ stable sets.
context_text: This more general conjecture was proposed by Sands, Sauer and Woodrow in their seminal 1982 paper. As a special case it implies the stable marriage theorem. It has been investigated in [1, 2, 6, 7] but remains open in general.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture for the special case of digraphs with bounded stability number, using the fact that the fractional domination of vertices by stable sets is at most 2 when the stability number is bounded.
paper_title: A proof of the Erdős-Sands-Sauer-Woodrow conjecture
